methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in AI & Software. Range: $9.7B - $300.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

private.name: OpenAI
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Private), post-money $300B+

private.name: Anthropic
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Private), post-money $183B

private.name: Anysphere

private.name: Neuralink
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Private), post-money $9.7B

data.narrative:
Groq is a private-stage AI & Software company founded by Jonathan Ross, currently valued at $7B with $3.89B in total capital raised. The valuation assessment draws on a comparable universe of 4 same-sector, same-stage private peers (OpenAI at $300B, Anthropic at $183B, Anysphere at $29.3B, and Neuralink at $9.7B), supplemented by reference to 1 public comparable (Palantir Technologies).

The peer-median method is the applicable triangulation approach given data availability. The median valuation across the 4-company peer set is $106.2B, with a range spanning $9.7B to $300B. This method reflects current market-clearing prices for late-stage AI companies but acknowledges significant dispersion driven by model capability, commercialization stage, and investor sentiment. Groq's current $7B valuation sits materially below the peer median, suggesting either a valuation discount relative to comparables or structural differences in business model, revenue traction, or investor confidence relative to the broader cohort.

The triangulated range derived from the peer-median method supports a valuation band of $90.2B (low) to $122.1B (high), with a central estimate of $106.2B. This analysis is constrained by reliance on a single method and a limited peer set with wide internal dispersion. Key uncertainties include Groq's revenue generation relative to peers, the sustainability of current private-market AI valuations, and market-specific factors affecting inference-acceleration hardware versus foundation models.
